---
title: "R Visualisation with ggplot2"
author: "Priit Adler, Nurlan Kerimov"
date: "2026-04-02"
output: html_document
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: 80
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(knitr)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(patchwork)  # for combining multiple plots into one layout
library(tibble)

opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)

cars <- mtcars %>%
  rownames_to_column(var = "car") %>%   # move row names (car models) into a real column
  mutate(
    cyl = factor(cyl),                  # treat cylinder count as a category, not a number
    gear = factor(gear),                # same for number of gears
    am = factor(am, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Automatic", "Manual"))  # give 0/1 readable labels
  )
```

## About this course

This course focuses on data visualisation with `ggplot2`, a popular R package
for building plots layer by layer. It is one of the most widely used and
powerful graphics packages in the R ecosystem. By learning `ggplot2`, you will
gain the ability to create professional, publication-quality plots that are
essential for data analysis and communication.

## Prerequisites

Before starting this session, learners should already be comfortable with:

-   basic R and RStudio use
-   running code chunks in R Markdown
-   tidy data
-   factors
-   categorical and continuous variables
-   small table operations such as `select()`, `filter()`, `mutate()`,
    `group_by()`, and `summarise()`

If you want a quick refresher on those ideas, use the `2026-04-02-R-warmup.Rmd`
document first.

## Data

We need data in order to visualise it. In `ggplot2`, data is the input that
tells the plot, "this is the table we will use to create the visualisation."
Before making a chart, it helps to know what the rows represent and what kind of
variables the columns contain.

Throughout this course, we use a slightly modified version of the built-in
`mtcars` dataset — see the `cars` dataframe below.

```{r}
glimpse(cars)
```

The main variables we will reuse are:

-   `mpg`: miles per gallon
-   `wt`: car weight in 1000 lb
-   `hp`: horsepower
-   `cyl`, `gear`, and `am`: categories that are useful for grouping and colour

Good plots start with understanding the dataset: what each row represents, which
variables are continuous, and which variables should be treated as categories.

For the rest of the course, we use the cleaned dataset `cars` rather than the
raw `mtcars` table.

## Your first plot

Before we break a plot into parts, it helps to see one complete example.

```{r}
ggplot(cars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = am)) +
  geom_point(size = 3)
```

Don't worry about the details yet — we will cover each piece step by step. For
now, notice three things:

-   we gave `ggplot()` some data (`cars`)
-   we told it what to put on each axis and how to colour the points (`aes(...)`)
-   we chose a shape to draw (`geom_point()`)

Everything else in this course builds on these three ideas.

## Grammar of graphics

![](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/articles/ggplot2_files/figure-html/overview_graphic-1.png)

Source: <https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/articles/ggplot2.html>

The `ggplot2` introduction describes a plot as seven composable parts:

-   data: the table we want to visualise
-   mapping: how variables are linked to aesthetics such as x, y, colour, or
    size
-   layers: what gets drawn, including the geom, the statistical transformation,
    and the position adjustment
-   scales: how values become axes, legend entries, colours, or sizes
-   facets: ways to split a dataset into multiple small panels
-   coordinates: the system that interprets position, such as Cartesian
    coordinates
-   theme: the non-data styling around the plot

Here is how the first plot we saw maps to these grammar components:

```r
ggplot(cars,                          # data
       aes(x = wt, y = mpg,          # mapping
           color = am)) +
  geom_point(size = 3)               # layer (geom)
```

The remaining components — scales, labels, and theme — use sensible defaults
but can be customised, as we will see in later sections.

This course keeps that full picture in mind, but we will focus on the parts that
beginners need most often.

## Mapping

Mapping connects variables in the dataset to what we see on screen. It tells
`ggplot2` which column should control the x-axis, the y-axis, the colour, the
size, or another visual property. Common aesthetics include `x`, `y`, `color`,
`fill`, `shape`, and `size`.

```{r}
ggplot(cars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = am)) +  # wt -> x, mpg -> y, am -> colour
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(color = "Transmission")  # labs() adds readable labels; we cover it in detail later
```

Here, `wt` is mapped to the x-axis, `mpg` to the y-axis, and `am` to colour.

In practice, mapping means asking questions like:

-   which variable goes on the x-axis?
-   which variable goes on the y-axis?
-   should colour represent a category or a number?

### Mapping versus setting

When an aesthetic belongs to the data, put it inside `aes()`. When you want a
fixed value, set it outside `aes()`.

```{r}
mapped_plot <- ggplot(cars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = am)) +  # color inside aes() = data-driven
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Colour mapped to a variable", color = "Transmission")

mapped_plot
```

```{r}
set_plot <- ggplot(cars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue") +  # color outside aes() = fixed value for all points
  labs(title = "Colour set to a fixed value")

set_plot
```

You can also map more than one aesthetic at the same time.

```{r}
ggplot(cars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = am, shape = cyl)) +  # two aesthetics mapped at once
  geom_point(size = 3)
```

### Task

Create a scatter plot of `disp` vs `hp`, map `color` to `cyl`, and set point
size to `3`.

```{r}
# Your code here

```

Expected result: a scatter plot where cylinder groups are distinguished by
colour while point size stays fixed.

Note: this task is useful for checking whether you understand the
difference between mapping a variable and setting a constant.

Additional reading:
[ggplot2 geoms reference](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/index.html#geoms),
[From Data to Viz](https://data-to-viz.com/).

## Layers

Layers are where the plot becomes visible. In `ggplot2`, a layer can include a
geom, a statistical transformation, and a position adjustment. In simple terms,
layers tell `ggplot2` what to draw on top of the data, such as points, bars,
boxes, or smooth lines.

### Choosing geoms

Different geoms are useful for different variable types. With the same dataset,
we can make several valid plots by changing only the geom.

```{r}
scatter_geom <- ggplot(cars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 3) +
  labs(title = "geom_point()")

scatter_geom
```

```{r}
hist_geom <- ggplot(cars, aes(x = mpg)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 2, fill = "tomato", color = "white") +  # binwidth controls bar width; color is bar border
  labs(title = "geom_histogram()")

hist_geom
```

Note: `color` controls outlines and the colour of points and lines. `fill`
controls the inside area of shapes like bars, boxes, and tiles. You will see
this distinction throughout the course.

```{r}
bar_geom <- ggplot(cars, aes(x = cyl)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "grey40") +   # geom_bar() counts rows per category automatically
  labs(title = "geom_bar()", x = "Cylinders")

bar_geom
```

```{r}
box_geom <- ggplot(cars, aes(x = cyl, y = mpg)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "khaki") +
  labs(title = "geom_boxplot()", x = "Cylinders")

box_geom
```

```{r}
line_geom <- ggplot(cars %>% arrange(wt), aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "geom_line()")

line_geom
```

`geom_line()` connects observations in the order they appear in the data. Here
we first sort the data by weight with `arrange(wt)` so that the line runs from
left to right. 

As a rule of thumb:

-   use scatter plots for two continuous variables
-   use histograms for one continuous variable
-   use bar plots for counts of categories
-   use box plots when comparing a continuous variable across categories
-   use line plots to show trends across an ordered variable

### Adding more than one layer

One of the strengths of `ggplot2` is that you can add more than one layer to the
same plot.

Lets check the difference between a line plot with and without points (as above):
```{r}
line_geom <- ggplot(cars %>% arrange(wt), aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 2) + 
  #Adding `geom_point()` on top makes individual cars visible.
  labs(title = "geom_line()")

line_geom
```


### Box plot with jittered points

```{r}
ggplot(cars, aes(x = gear, y = mpg)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "grey85", outlier.shape = NA) +  # hide outlier dots so jitter shows all cars
  geom_jitter(aes(color = am), width = 0.15, size = 2.5, alpha = 0.85) +  # color = am only applies to this geom
  labs(x = "Gears", y = "Miles per gallon", color = "Transmission") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Note: Aesthetics defined in `ggplot(aes(...))` are inherited by every layer. However,
each geom can add or override aesthetics in its own `aes()` call. In the example
below, `x = gear` and `y = mpg` are shared by both layers, but only
`geom_jitter()` maps `color = am` — so the box plots stay a single colour while
the points are coloured by transmission.


The jittered points show the individual cars, while the box plot shows the
summary distribution for each gear group.

### Scatter plot with a trend line and labels

```{r}
ggplot(cars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = am), size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +  # lm = linear trend line; se = FALSE hides confidence ribbon
  geom_text(
    data = cars %>% filter(mpg > 30 | wt > 5),  # only label the extreme/interesting cars
    aes(label = car),
    check_overlap = TRUE,  # skip labels that would overlap each other
    nudge_y = 1,           # shift labels slightly above their points
    size = 3
  ) +
  labs(color = "Transmission")
```

### Task

Build a plot of `hp` by `cyl` that combines `geom_boxplot()` and
`geom_jitter()`. Colour the points by `gear`.

```{r}
# Your code here

```

Expected result: a layered plot that shows both the horsepower distribution per
cylinder group and the individual observations coloured by number of gears.

## Scales

Scales control how values are translated into positions, colours, fills, and
labels. They help turn raw values into readable axes and legends, and they let
us control breaks, labels, ordering, and colour palettes.

### Numeric scales

Numeric scales are useful when you want cleaner breaks or more readable axes.

```{r}
numeric_default <- ggplot(cars, aes(x = hp, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "grey40") +
  labs(title = "Default numeric scales")

numeric_default
```

`scale_x_continuous()` and `scale_y_continuous()` let you control how numeric
axes behave. The most common use is the `breaks` argument, which sets where the
tick marks appear. Here we use `seq()` to generate evenly spaced positions.

```{r}
numeric_scaled <- ggplot(cars, aes(x = hp, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "grey40") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(50, 350, by = 50)) +  # seq() generates tick positions: 50, 100, 150 ...
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(10, 35, by = 5)) +
  labs(title = "Custom numeric breaks")

numeric_scaled
```

### Discrete scales and factor order

Ordering categories can change the story a plot tells. Here the first box plot
uses the default factor order, while the second sets a new order manually.

```{r}
default_order_plot <- ggplot(cars, aes(x = factor(cyl), y = mpg)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "grey80") +
  labs(title = "Default order", x = "Cylinders")

default_order_plot
```

```{r}
reordered_plot <- ggplot(cars, aes(x = factor(cyl, levels = c("8", "6", "4")), y = mpg)) +  # set the category order directly
  geom_boxplot(fill = "tomato") +
  labs(title = "Manually reordered", x = "Cylinders")

reordered_plot
```

This is the point where factor handling matters directly for visualisation.

Note: this example now changes the x-axis order visibly from `4, 6, 8`
to `8, 6, 4`, which makes the reason for reordering much clearer.

You can also control discrete axes with `scale_x_discrete()`. The `limits`
argument sets the order of categories, and the `labels` argument renames them.

```{r}
ggplot(cars, aes(x = cyl, y = mpg)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "orchid") +
  scale_x_discrete(
    limits = c("8", "6", "4"),                       # set the display order
    labels = c("8-cyl", "6-cyl", "4-cyl")            # rename the tick labels
  ) +
  labs(title = "Relabelled with scale_x_discrete()", x = "Cylinders")
```

### Colour scales and fill scales

Use `color` for points and lines. Use `fill` for objects with an inside area,
such as bars, tiles, and box plots.

```{r}
color_scale_plot <- ggplot(cars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = hp)) +
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "skyblue", high = "firebrick") +  # low hp = blue, high hp = red
  labs(title = "Continuous colour scale", color = "Horsepower")

color_scale_plot
```

```{r}
mean_mpg_by_cyl_gear <- cars %>%
  group_by(cyl, gear) %>%
  summarise(mean_mpg = mean(mpg), .groups = "drop")  # .groups = "drop" removes grouping after summarise
```

```{r}
fill_scale_plot <- ggplot(mean_mpg_by_cyl_gear, aes(x = cyl, y = gear, fill = mean_mpg)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.8) +                          # color here is the tile border, not fill
  geom_text(aes(label = round(mean_mpg, 1)), color = "white", fontface = "bold") +  # overlay value labels on tiles
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "khaki1", high = "darkgreen") +
  labs(
    title = "Continuous fill scale",
    x = "Cylinders",
    y = "Gears",
    fill = "Mean mpg"
  )

fill_scale_plot
```

Note: this section deliberately separates `scale_color_gradient()` and
`scale_fill_gradient()` so you can see the correct use case for each one.

### Task

Create a scatter plot of `wt` versus `mpg` where `hp` is mapped to colour.
Use `scale_color_gradient()` to set a gradient from `"gold"` to `"purple"`,
and set custom x-axis breaks at every 1000 lb with `scale_x_continuous()`.

```{r}
# Your code here

```

Expected result: a scatter plot with a gold-to-purple colour gradient and
evenly spaced x-axis ticks.

## Labels and titles

Now that you know how to build a plot from data, mapping, layers, and scales,
it is time to add human-readable labels. You have already seen `labs()` used
in earlier examples. Here is a closer look at what it can do.

`labs()` sets the title, subtitle, caption, axis names, and legend titles for
a plot.

```{r}
ggplot(cars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = am)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Fuel efficiency and car weight",
    subtitle = "Heavier cars tend to use more fuel",
    x = "Weight (1000 lb)",
    y = "Miles per gallon",
    color = "Transmission",
    caption = "Data source: mtcars"
  )
```

Each argument in `labs()` targets a specific part of the plot:

-   `title` and `subtitle` appear at the top
-   `x` and `y` rename the axis labels
-   `color`, `fill`, `shape`, or `size` rename the corresponding legend
-   `caption` adds a note at the bottom right

## Theme

Polishing matters. A plot becomes easier to read when it has a clear title,
useful axis labels, and a consistent theme. The theme controls the visual style
around the data, such as fonts, grid lines, legend position, and background
appearance.

```{r}
styled_plot <- ggplot(cars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = am)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Heavier cars tend to have lower fuel efficiency",
    subtitle = "Transmission highlights one part of the pattern in mtcars",
    x = "Weight (1000 lb)",
    y = "Miles per gallon",
    color = "Transmission"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +  # clean background; base_size scales all text proportionally
  theme(legend.position = "top")   # move legend above the plot

styled_plot
```

We will not cover coordinates or native faceting functions (such as
`facet_wrap()` or `facet_grid()`) in this course. However, to show how different
plots can be combined, we will use the `patchwork` package to arrange multiple
independent plots into a single layout.

## Patchwork

The `patchwork` package makes it easy to combine several ggplots into one
layout. Instead of relying on native faceting in this course, we use `patchwork`
to place separate plots next to each other or stack them into a single figure.

The two basic operators are:

-   `|` places plots side by side
-   `/` stacks plots vertically

```{r}
scatter_geom | hist_geom  # side by side
```

```{r}
scatter_geom / box_geom  # stacked
```

You can combine both operators and add annotations to build more complex
layouts.

```{r}
dashboard_plot <- (styled_plot / (reordered_plot | fill_scale_plot)) +  # / stacks rows; inner | places side by side
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Three views of the mtcars dataset",
    subtitle = "Scatter plot, reordered box plot, and summary heatmap",
    caption = "Data source: mtcars"
  ) +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect")  # merge duplicate legends into one shared legend

dashboard_plot
```

## Exporting plots with `ggsave()`

`ggsave()` writes a plot to a file. In this example we save to temporary files
so that knitting the document does not clutter the course folder.

```{r}
png_file <- tempfile("mtcars-plot-", fileext = ".png")  # tempfile() creates a safe throwaway path
pdf_file <- tempfile("mtcars-plot-", fileext = ".pdf")

ggsave(filename = png_file, plot = styled_plot, width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 300)  # dpi = resolution; 300 is print quality
ggsave(filename = pdf_file, plot = styled_plot, width = 8, height = 5)             # PDF is vector so dpi is irrelevant

png_file  # print the path so you can inspect the saved file
pdf_file
```

When you make your own report, replace `tempfile()` with a real filename such as
`"my-figure.png"`.

## Final task

Create a two-panel figure about fuel efficiency in `mtcars`.

Requirements:

-   one panel should use two continuous variables
-   one panel should use a categorical x-axis
-   choose at least one scale intentionally
-   add meaningful labels or a title

```{r}
# Your code here

```

Expected result: a compact two-panel figure that uses both continuous and
categorical views of the same topic.

Note: it is good practice to explain why you chose your scale and
your plot types, not only how you coded them.
